1:
"And it came to pass at that time, that Judah went down from his brethren, and turned in to a certain Adullamite, whose name was Hiras." The witnesses note Judah going down from his brethren to Adullam. On "Judah went down from his brethren," the grammatical voices note the departure. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Judah went down from his brethren, and turned in to a certain Adullamite, whose name was Hirah" — Judah separated himself from his brethren (the family of Jacob) and went down to dwell with an Adullamite (a Canaanite of Adullam); the going "down" (morally as well as geographically); the departure from the covenant household into Canaanite company; the setting for the sad history of the chapter.

On "at that time," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the placement. Henry and Calvin note that this history is set "at that time" (about the time of Joseph's sale, ch. 37) — the chapter interrupting the Joseph narrative; the contrast (the fathers note) between Judah's degradation among the Canaanites (ch. 38) and Joseph's purity in Egypt (ch. 39); the dark interlude.

On "turned in to a certain Adullamite," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the company: "turned in to a certain Adullamite, whose name was Hirah" — Judah consorted with a Canaanite (Hirah); the mingling with the people of the land (the very thing the patriarchs shunned, 24:3); the descent that led to the Canaanite marriage (v.2) and the sad events following; the warning of Judah's worldly turn. The verse displays Judah going down from his brethren to Adullam.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the departure — "Judah went down from his brethren, and turned in to a certain Adullamite," Judah's descent into Canaanite company (Keil, Gill); "at that time," the placement (the contrast with Joseph, ch. 39, Henry, Calvin); "turned in to a certain Adullamite," the mingling with the people of the land (24:3, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the departed Judah, who marries a Canaanite (v.2).
2:

On "Judah saw there a daughter of a certain Canaanite, whose name was Shuah," the grammatical voices note the marriage. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Judah saw there a daughter of a certain Canaanite, whose name was Shuah; and he took her" — Judah married the daughter of Shua, a Canaanite (the daughter herself unnamed, called "Bath-shua," daughter of Shua, 1 Chronicles 2:3); the Canaanite marriage (the very thing Abraham and Isaac had forbidden, 24:3; 28:1); the worldly match; the descent continued.

On "a daughter of a certain Canaanite," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the fault. Calvin and Gill note the Canaanite marriage — Judah, unlike his fathers (who sought wives of the kindred, chs. 24, 28), married a daughter of Canaan; the mingling with the accursed line; the fault that bred the sad history (the wicked sons, vv.7, 10); the warning against unequal, worldly marriage.

On "he took her, and went in unto her," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the consequence: "he took her, and went in unto her" — the marriage consummated; the Canaanite wife who bore Er, Onan, and Shelah (vv.3-5); the union that brought forth the wicked sons; the sad fruit of the worldly match. The verse displays Judah marrying a Canaanite woman.

The thin comment matches the verse. 3:
"And she conceived, and bare a son; and he called his name Er." The very brief verse notes the birth of Er. The grammatical voices note the son. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "she conceived, and bare a son; and he called his name Er" — Judah's Canaanite wife bore a first son, Er ("watcher," or "awake"); the firstborn of Judah; the son who would prove wicked (v.7); the beginning of the sad family history.

On "bare a son," the witnesses note the birth. Gill notes the first son, Er — Judah's firstborn by the Canaanite wife; the son who would marry Tamar (v.6) and be slain for his wickedness (v.7).

On "he called his name Er," the grammatical-historical note the name: "he called his name Er" — the firstborn named Er; the son whose wickedness (v.7) would leave Tamar a childless widow; the beginning of the levirate history (vv.8-11). The verse displays the birth of Er.

4:
"And she conceived again, and bare a son; and she called his name Onan." The very brief verse notes the birth of Onan. The grammatical voices note the son. Gill notes that "she conceived again, and bare a son; and she called his name Onan" — Judah's wife bore a second son, Onan ("strong," or "vigorous"); the second son of Judah; the son who would sin in the levirate duty and be slain (vv.8-10); the family history continuing.

On "bare a son," the witnesses note the birth. Gill notes the second son, Onan — Judah's second by the Canaanite wife; the son who would refuse the levirate duty to Tamar (vv.8-9) and be slain (v.10).

Like his elder brother Er, this son too would prove wicked in the sight of the LORD and be cut off in his sin (Genesis 38:10), so that of Judah's three sons by the Canaanite woman two were quickly taken in judgment, and only Shelah remained -- a dark providence overshadowing the house of Judah before the line of promise was carried on through Tamar (Genesis 38:29). The verse displays the wife: bearing. The verse displays the Onan: named. The verse displays the family: growing. 5:

On "called his name Shelah," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the name. Clarke and Ellicott note the third son, Shelah — the one Judah promised to Tamar but withheld (v.11); the son who survived (unlike Er and Onan); the head of a family of Judah (the Shelanites, Numbers 26:20).

On "he was at Chezib, when she bare him," the grammatical-historical note the place: "he was at Chezib, when she bare him" — the place of Shelah's birth (Chezib/Achzib); the notice of the place; the third son who would figure in the levirate history (vv.11, 14, 26). The verse displays the birth of Shelah.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the son — "she yet again conceived, and bare a son; and called his name Shelah," Judah's third son (v.11, Ellicott, Gill); "Shelah," the withheld son (Clarke, Ellicott); "he was at Chezib, when she bare him," the place (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the born Shelah. The verse marks the son: Shelah. The verse displays the third: of Judah. The verse displays the Chezib: the place. The verse displays the withholding: to come. 6:
"And Judah took a wife for Er his firstborn, whose name was Tamar." The very brief verse notes Judah taking Tamar as wife for Er. The grammatical voices note the marriage. Gill notes that "Judah took a wife for Er his firstborn, whose name was Tamar" — Judah, as father, chose a wife for his firstborn Er: Tamar ("palm tree"); the marriage arranged by the father (the custom); Tamar (probably a Canaanite, or by some an Aramean) given to Er; the wife who would be widowed by Er's death (v.7) and become the central figure of the chapter.

On "a wife for Er his firstborn," the witnesses note the firstborn. Gill notes that Tamar was married to Er, Judah's firstborn (v.3) — the wife of the eldest son; the marriage that, by Er's wickedness and death (v.7), would leave Tamar a childless widow and set the levirate history in motion (vv.8-11).

On "whose name was Tamar," the grammatical-historical note the woman: "whose name was Tamar" — the woman whose persistence (and stratagem, vv.14-26) would secure her the seed denied her, and who would become an ancestress of David and of Christ (Matthew 1:3, "Judas begat Phares and Zara of Thamar"); the notable figure introduced. The verse displays Judah taking Tamar as wife for Er.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the marriage — "Judah took a wife for Er his firstborn, whose name was Tamar," the marriage arranged (v.3, Gill); "a wife for Er his firstborn," the eldest son's wife (vv.7-8, Gill); "Tamar," the ancestress of Christ (Matthew 1:3, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the marrying Judah. The verse marks the wife: Tamar. The verse displays the Er: the firstborn. The verse displays the marriage: arranged. The verse displays the widow: to be. The marriage displays Tamar given to Er. The verse displays the Judah: arranging. The verse displays the Tamar: married. The verse displays the history: begun. 7:
"And Er, Judah's firstborn, was wicked in the sight of the LORD; and the LORD slew him." The witnesses note Er's wickedness and the LORD slaying him. On "Er, Judah's firstborn, was wicked in the sight of the LORD," the grammatical voices note the wickedness. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Er, Judah's firstborn, was wicked in the sight of the LORD" — Er was wicked (the particular sin not named, but grievous in God's sight); the firstborn of Judah given to evil (the fruit of the Canaanite mother and the worldly home); the wickedness that brought God's judgment.

On "wicked in the sight of the LORD," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the judgment. Calvin and Gill note that Er was wicked "in the sight of the LORD" — his sin open before God (whatever its precise form, some conjecture an unnatural sin, as Onan's, v.9); the divine notice of the wickedness; the judgment of God on the evil firstborn.

On "the LORD slew him," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the death: "the LORD slew him" — God put Er to death for his wickedness (a direct judgment, as later Nadab and Abihu, Leviticus 10:2; Ananias, Acts 5:5); the sovereign judgment on sin; Er dying childless, leaving Tamar a widow (the occasion of the levirate, v.8); the warning against wickedness. The verse displays Er's wickedness and the LORD slaying him.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the wickedness — "Er, Judah's firstborn, was wicked in the sight of the LORD," the evil firstborn (Keil, Gill); "wicked in the sight of the LORD," the sin open before God (Calvin, Gill); "the LORD slew him," the divine judgment (Leviticus 10:2; Acts 5:5, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the slain Er, and Onan's levirate duty (v.8).
8:
"And Judah said unto Onan, Go in unto thy brother's wife, and marry her, and raise up seed to thy brother." The witnesses note Judah charging Onan with the levirate duty. On "Go in unto thy brother's wife, and marry her," the grammatical voices note the duty. Ellicott and Gill note that "Judah said unto Onan, Go in unto thy brother's wife, and marry her, and raise up seed to thy brother" — Judah charged Onan to perform the levirate duty: to marry Tamar (his dead brother's widow) and raise up seed (children) to the deceased brother's name; the custom of the levirate (the brother marrying the childless widow to continue the dead brother's line and name), later codified in the law (Deuteronomy 25:5-10; Matthew 22:24); the duty owed to the dead.

On "marry her," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the levirate. Calvin and Gill note the levirate marriage — the brother (or near kinsman) marrying the widow to raise seed to the dead (the preservation of the family name and inheritance); the ancient custom (preceding the Mosaic law); the duty charged on Onan.

On "raise up seed to thy brother," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the purpose: "raise up seed to thy brother" — the firstborn of the levirate union to be reckoned to the dead brother (his name and line continued, Deuteronomy 25:6); the purpose of the duty; the seed that Onan would selfishly refuse (v.9); the custom that figures in Ruth (4:5-10) and the line of David. The verse displays Judah charging Onan with the levirate duty.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the duty — "Go in unto thy brother's wife, and marry her, and raise up seed to thy brother," the levirate charge (Deuteronomy 25:5, Ellicott, Gill); "marry her," the levirate marriage (Calvin, Gill); "raise up seed to thy brother," the purpose (Deuteronomy 25:6; Ruth 4:5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charged Onan, who sins (vv.9-10).
9:
"And Onan knew that the seed should not be his; and it came to pass, when he went in unto his brother's wife, that he spilled it on the ground, lest that he should give seed to his brother." The witnesses note Onan's refusal of the levirate seed — the crux of his sin. On "Onan knew that the seed should not be his," the grammatical voices note the motive. Gill and Clarke note that "Onan knew that the seed should not be his" — Onan knew that the child of the levirate union would be reckoned to his dead brother (Er), not to himself (Deuteronomy 25:6); the firstborn would bear the dead brother's name and inheritance, not Onan's; the selfish calculation; the motive of his sin.

On "he spilled it on the ground, lest that he should give seed to his brother," interpreters expound the sin. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note that Onan, though going in to Tamar (taking the pleasure of the marriage), "spilled it on the ground" — frustrating the purpose of the levirate, that he might not raise up seed to his brother; the sin bears a double aspect, and the witnesses weigh it: (a) the malicious refusal of the levirate duty — Onan's covetous, envious unwillingness to raise up seed to his dead brother (grudging the inheritance, perhaps coveting it himself); the breach of the duty owed to the dead and to Tamar; the selfish, fraudulent act (taking the marriage but denying its end); (b) the act itself — the wasting/spilling of the seed (whence the term "onanism") — an unnatural, abominable deed; the dominant reading holds the chief sin to be the malicious, selfish refusal (the heart that grudged his brother seed), aggravated by the foul means; in either aspect a grievous sin against nature, his brother, and God.

On the deed, the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the wickedness: Onan's act — taking the pleasure but refusing the duty, and wasting the seed — was wicked in God's sight (v.10); the selfishness, fraud, and unnatural deed combined; the sin that displeased the LORD (v.10) and brought his death. The verse displays Onan's refusal of the levirate seed.

The witnesses agree the substance: Onan, knowing the seed would not be reckoned his, spilled it to avoid raising seed to his brother (Gill, Calvin, Clarke). His sin bears a crux: the malicious, selfish refusal of the levirate duty (grudging his dead brother seed and inheritance) — the chief sin (the dominant reading) — aggravated by the unnatural act itself (the wasting of the seed, whence "onanism"); a grievous sin against his brother, Tamar, nature, and God, which displeased the LORD (v.10). The verse displays the sinning Onan, slain by God (v.10).

11:
"Then said Judah to Tamar his daughter in law, Remain a widow at thy father's house, till Shelah my son be grown: for he said, Lest peradventure he die also, as his brethren did. And she went and dwelt in her father's house." The witnesses note Judah sending Tamar home, withholding Shelah. On "Remain a widow at thy father's house, till Shelah my son be grown," the grammatical voices note the deferral. Ellicott and Gill note that "Judah said to Tamar... Remain a widow at thy father's house, till Shelah my son be grown" — Judah sent Tamar to her father's house as a widow, with the promise that she should have Shelah (his third son) when he was grown; the levirate duty deferred (to Shelah); the seeming promise; the widow sent home to wait.

Calvin and Gill note the secret motive — Judah feared "lest peradventure he die also, as his brethren did" (Er and Onan, vv.7, 10); Judah superstitiously blaming Tamar (as if she were the cause of his sons' deaths, not their own wickedness); the unjust suspicion; the real intent to withhold Shelah (not to give him at all, as v.14, 26 show); the deceitful deferral.

On "she went and dwelt in her father's house," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the waiting: "she went and dwelt in her father's house" — Tamar obeyed, returning to her father's house as a widow, awaiting Shelah; the long wait (Shelah grown, yet not given, v.14); the wrong done to Tamar (denied her levirate right); the occasion of her stratagem (vv.13-19). The verse displays Judah sending Tamar home, withholding Shelah.

12:

The witnesses note the death of Judah's wife and his going to the sheepshearing. On "the daughter of Shuah Judah's wife died," the grammatical voices note the death. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "in process of time the daughter of Shuah Judah's wife died; and Judah was comforted" — Judah's Canaanite wife (v.2) died, and after the time of mourning Judah was comforted (his grief past); the setting for the events with Tamar (Judah now a widower); the passage of time (Shelah now grown, v.14).

On "Judah was comforted, and went up unto his sheepshearers to Timnath," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the occasion. Henry and Ellicott note that Judah, comforted, went up to Timnath to his sheepshearers (the sheepshearing a time of feasting and festivity, as 1 Samuel 25:4-8; 2 Samuel 13:23); the occasion for the journey; the festive time; the setting for Tamar's stratagem (vv.13-19).

On "he and his friend Hirah the Adullamite," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the company: "he and his friend Hirah the Adullamite" — Judah with his Canaanite friend (v.1); the worldly company; the journey to the sheepshearing; the occasion Tamar would use (learning of it, v.13). 13:
"And it was told Tamar, saying, Behold thy father in law goeth up to Timnath to shear his sheep." The very brief verse notes Tamar learning of Judah's journey. The grammatical voices note the news. 26:

Gill and Clarke note that "Judah acknowledged them" — Judah recognized the tokens (the signet, bracelets, staff, v.25) as his own; the proof undeniable; the conviction; the patriarch owning the truth (rather than denying or destroying the evidence); the honest acknowledgment.

On "She hath been more righteous than I," interpreters expound the confession. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note Judah's words — "She hath been more righteous than I; because that I gave her not to Shelah my son": Judah confessed that Tamar was "more righteous" than he — not that her act (the deceit and harlotry) was righteous (the witnesses are careful: her means were faulty), but that she was less to blame than he, who had wronged her by withholding Shelah (v.11) and so driving her to the stratagem; Judah owning his greater fault (the breach of the levirate duty, and his own sin with the supposed harlot); the honest, humbling confession of the patriarch (a mark of grace, that he confessed rather than excused, before all); the self-condemnation (his own sentence, v.24, turned on himself); some take "more righteous" as her cause being just (the levirate seed her right), while his withholding was unjust.
